topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom air was thick with forced merriment and the cloying scent of expensive perfume. I leaned against a marble pillar, nursing a lukewarm champagne, my gaze deliberately fixed on the glittering ceiling. Then, there he was. Liam. He was standing near the buffet, laughing with a group of people I vaguely recognized—people who clearly enjoyed his company. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. Seeing him, looking polished and utterly unbothered, sent a familiar, sour knot tightening in my chest. I watched him move, a practiced grace I used to envy, and felt a distinct urge to look away, to erase him from my immediate visual field. When our eyes finally met across the crowded room, there was a flicker—a brief acknowledgment—and I offered nothing back but a tight, dismissive narrowing of my eyes before turning my attention back to the architecture.

[story 2]

I had made a conscious effort to appear engaged, nodding politely at the toasts, but every time Liam’s voice carried across the room, a distinct chill seemed to run down my spine. He looked exactly the same—the easy smile, the tailored suit—but underneath the surface, I could sense a distance that hadn't been there before. When he navigated through the crowd toward my section, I kept my posture rigid, arms crossed over my chest. I met his gaze for a fraction of a second, and the expression on my face was carefully blank, a mask of polite indifference. I allowed my eyes to sweep over him, cataloging the superficiality of the moment, before deliberately shifting my focus to a distant painting. The way he seemed to inhabit the space, owning it effortlessly, only amplified the feeling of irrelevance I was cultivating internally.

[story 3]

The music swelled, a saccharine string arrangement that grated on my nerves. I was trying to enjoy the spectacle, but the proximity of Liam was an unwelcome intrusion. He caught my eye from across the dance floor, and for a moment, the noise of the party seemed to recede. I felt a sharp, internal tightening in my throat, a sudden urge to pull my shoulders higher, to create an invisible barrier between us. I didn't smile. I didn't even twitch. I just held his stare for a beat too long, letting the silence between us speak volumes—a silent declaration that whatever connection we once shared was long since dismantled, replaced by this sterile, inconvenient recognition. Then, I turned my back sharply, walking toward the edge of the room, needing distance from that familiar, irritating presence.
